segments.purgatorio-4-g23: The verbs vassi and discendesi carry the enclitic -si, which in medieval/poetic Italian makes them impersonal or reflexive in a general sense ('one goes', 'one descends'). Word order is subjectless+verb+place (Vassi in Sanleo = 'One goes to San Leo'). The preposition in marks location/direction.
segments.purgatorio-4-g24: Montasi is another impersonal/reflexive form ('one mounts' or 'people climb'); su indicates upward motion. The sequence in Bismantova e ’n Cacume lists two places/points reached; the apostrophized ’n is a contracted preposition (in) before a vowel. Con esso i piè means 'with it (the rock) the feet' — here esso refers back to the rock and i piè ('the feet') completes the spatial image.
segments.purgatorio-4-g25: Ma qui convien ch’om voli is a sudden general necessity: convien is an impersonal present meaning 'it is necessary', and the clause ch’om voli is a subordinate clause introduced by che (here contracted 'ch’'), with voli intended as a subjunctive/infinitival sense 'that one fly/should fly'. Qui 'here' localizes the necessity.
segments.purgatorio-4-g26: Dico begins a glossing remark 'I say' (present tense). Con l’ale snelle e con le piume strings two instrumental phrases: con + plural nouns (ale 'wings', piume 'feathers') with the adjective snelle 'nimble/slender' postposed, a normal poetic word order. Del gran disio is a genitive phrase 'of great desire' modifying piume.
segments.purgatorio-4-g27: Di retro a quel condotto is a prepositional phrase meaning 'behind that conduct/guide' (di retro = 'from behind' or 'behind'). Che introduces a relative clause whose verb sequence speranza mi dava e facea lume uses imperfect dava and facea to describe an ongoing past function: 'which hope gave me and made light' (dava = 'used to give' / 'gave'; facea = 'made' / 'gave off' light). Mi is the indirect object 'to me'.
segments.purgatorio-4-g28: Noi is the explicit subject 'we'. Salavam is an archaic/imperfect form meaning a continuous past action (here contextually 'we clambered/advanced'); per entro l' sasso rotto is a circumstantial phrase 'through/within the broken rock' (per + entro emphasizes movement through the interior). Rotto modifies sasso.
segments.purgatorio-4-g29: E d’ogne lato ne stringea lo stremo: the sequence d'ogne lato ('from every side') is an ablative-like phrase; ne is a clitic pronoun referring back to 'us/there' and here functions to mark the object affected by stringea ('pressed/tightened'). Stringea is imperfect 'pressed/was pressing', and lo stremo is 'the brink/edge' doing the pressing.
segments.purgatorio-4-g30: E piedi e man volea il suol di sotto lists two coordinated objects 'feet and hands' (piedi e man) followed by volea, an imperfect of volere meaning 'wanted/required' intransitively here 'the ground below required (that we use) feet and hands.' Il suol di sotto ('the soil/ground below') is the subject that 'wanted' them.
segments.purgatorio-4-g31: Poi che introduces a temporal clause 'after', and fummo is the perfect past 'we were' (noi fummo). In su l’orlo suppremo de l’alta ripa places the subject on the highest edge of the high bank; note poetic stacking of prepositions in su and in. Suppremo is used adjectivally here ('supreme/highest').
segments.purgatorio-4-g32: A la scoperta piaggia: a introduces motion or arrival 'to' and la scoperta piaggia is a noun phrase 'the uncovered/exposed slope' (scoperta = 'exposed/uncovered', piaggia = 'slope/shore'). Word order is determiner + adjective + noun in a compact poetic line.
segments.purgatorio-4-g33: «Maestro mio», is a vocative: Maestro (address) + mio 'my', a possessive used in vocative position meaning 'my master'.
segments.purgatorio-4-g34: Diss' io is the reduced poetic form of dissi io 'I said', with the apostrophe marking elision. This places the speech-mark immediately after the narration.
segments.purgatorio-4-g35: Che via faremo? is a direct question: che 'which/what', via 'way/route', and faremo is future 'shall we make/do' meaning 'what way shall we take?'. The inversion is typical in questions.
segments.purgatorio-4-g36: A single period segment: no words to analyze; it closes the speech/line in the manuscript.
segments.purgatorio-4-g114: A vocative address: O is the interjection calling attention, followed by dolce segnor mio (literally “sweet lord my”), where segnor is an archaic spelling of signore and mio follows the noun as a possessive. The whole phrase is direct address to the addressee.
segments.purgatorio-4-g115: diss' io is a narrative past—diss (short for dissi) is the passato remoto of dire (“to say”) with an elision marked by the apostrophe, and io is the explicit subject placed after the verb (typical of poetic narration).
segments.purgatorio-4-g116: This is an imperative + relative clause. adocchia is a 2nd-person singular imperative (an archaic/poetic form of ‘look’ or ‘notice’) commanding the addressee to observe; colui is the demonstrative ‘that one,’ followed by the relative che. mostra sé = ‘shows himself’ (mostra is present/indicative or literal ‘shows/appears’ and sé is the stressed reflexive pronoun). più negligente sets up a comparative; the following che ... fosse clause (introduced by che and se) expresses a hypothetical comparison: se is ‘if/as if’ and fosse is the imperfect subjunctive of essere (‘were’), so che se pigrizia fosse sua serocchia means roughly ‘than if laziness were his sister’ (serocchia = sister).
segments.purgatorio-4-g117: Allor si volse a noi uses passato remoto volse (from volgere) with the reflexive si to mean ‘he turned (himself) to us.’ puose mente is an archaic expression: puose (past historic of porre/ to put) + mente (‘mind’) meaning ‘he put/laid his mind (on us)’ or ‘he paid attention.’ Word order (verb + subject pronoun) is typical of narrative style.
segments.purgatorio-4-g118: movendo ’l viso is a present participle (movendo = ‘moving’) describing a simultaneous action; ’l is a contraction of il. pur su per la coscia is an adverbial phrase: pur ‘still/even’ + su ‘up’ + per la coscia ‘along/over the thigh,’ indicating the motion of his face upward along the thigh.
segments.purgatorio-4-g119: e disse is simply the conjunction e ‘and’ plus disse, the passato remoto of dire (‘he said’), introducing direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-4-g120: Or va tu sù is an imperative exhortation: Or = ‘now,’ va is a 2nd-person singular imperative (or informal present used as a command) of andare ‘go,’ tu is the emphasized subject ‘you,’ and sù is the adverb ‘up.’ The word order places the time adverb first for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-4-g121: che se’ valente: che here introduces a reason/explanation (‘for/since’), se’ is an archaic or poetic form of sei (2nd-person singular present of essere: ‘you are’), and valente is an adjective meaning ‘able’ or ‘capable,’ functioning as a predicate complement referring to tu.
segments.purgatorio-4-g122: This segment is a punctuation mark: the full stop ends the previous sentence/utterance. In the poem punctuation marks are treated as separate segments to mark prosodic or syntactic closure.
segments.purgatorio-4-g123: Conobbi allor chi era = 'I recognized then who he was.' 'Conobbi' is passato remoto of conoscere meaning 'I recognized/met'; 'allor' is an adverb 'then/thereupon'; 'chi era' is a subordinate clause 'who he was' with the verb 'era' (imperfect of essere) placed after the interrogative/relative pronoun 'chi'.
segments.purgatorio-4-g124: e quella angoscia che m’avacciava un poco ancor la lena = 'and that anguish which was quickening my breath still a little.' 'che' introduces a relative clause; 'm (m') is the clitic for 'to me'/'me' attached before the verb; 'avacciava' is the imperfect tense (3rd sing.) of avacciare meaning 'to quicken' or 'to hasten' and describes an ongoing past action; 'un poco ancor' = 'still a little'; 'la lena' ('the breath') is the direct object placed after the verb.
segments.purgatorio-4-g125: non m’impedì l’andare a lui; = 'did not prevent me from going to him.' 'Non' negates the verb; 'm' is the clitic object 'me' placed before the verb; 'imped� (impedì) ' is passato remoto (3rd sing.) of impedire 'to hinder/prevent'; 'l'andare' is a nominalized infinitive (the article + infinitive) functioning as the object 'the act of going'; 'a lui' indicates direction/toward him.
segments.purgatorio-4-g126: e poscia ch’a lui fu’ giunto, = 'and afterwards, when I had come to him.' 'poscia' = 'afterwards'; 'ch' (che) introduces a temporal clause 'when/that'; 'a lui' = 'to him'; 'fu' is the passato remoto (here used as the perfect auxiliary) and 'giunto' is the past participle of giungere, so together they express 'I had reached/arrived at him' (older poetic morphology often uses fu' for first-person perfect forms).
segments.purgatorio-4-g127: alzò la testa a pena, = 'he raised his head with difficulty/only scarcely.' 'Alzò' is passato remoto (3rd sing.) of alzare 'raised'; 'la testa' is the direct object; 'a pena' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'scarcely' or 'with difficulty' describing the manner of the action.
segments.purgatorio-4-g128: dicendo: is the gerund of dire meaning 'saying' and introduces the direct speech that follows; the gerund denotes an action contemporaneous with or immediately following the raising of the head.
segments.purgatorio-4-g129: «Hai ben veduto come ’l sole / da l’omero sinistro il carro mena?» = direct question: 'Have you well/clearly seen how the sun drives the chariot from the left shoulder?' 'Hai veduto' is present perfect (auxiliary avere in present + past participle) used here as a simple present perfect interrogative 'have you seen'; 'ben' = 'well/clearly'; 'come' = 'how/as' introducing the content of what to have seen; the rest is a descriptive clause where 'da l'omero sinistro' = 'from the left shoulder' and 'il carro mena' uses mena (3rd sing. of menare) 'leads/drives' with 'il carro' as subject.
segments.purgatorio-4-g53: Sì is an adverb meaning 'so' or 'thus' (emphatic). Spronaron is passato remoto, 3rd person plural of spronare ('they spurred on'); the enclitic mi before the verb is the indirect object 'me' (they spurred me on). Le parole sue is the direct object phrase 'his words' with the possessive sue following the noun and agreeing in gender/number.
segments.purgatorio-4-g54: Ch’ is the clitic form of che introducing a subordinate/result clause ('that'). The token i (here representing the archaic ïo) is the subject pronoun 'I' (archaic spelling) and does not carry an article sense in the provided options. Mi sforzai is passato remoto 1st person of sforzare(sì)/sforzarsi: 'I made an effort / I strained myself' with the reflexive/indirect pronoun mi. Carpando is a gerund (manner) from carpare, indicating the manner of the action ('grasping/seizing/holding on'), and appresso lui means 'close to him' or 'after him' (appresso = near/close to; lui is the tonic pronoun 'him'). Word order places the gerund after the main verb to express how the effort happened.
segments.purgatorio-4-g55: Tanto che is a result clause marker ('so much that'). '’l' is the elided article lo = 'the' before cinghio ('girdle, belt'). Sotto i piè is the locative phrase 'under the feet' (sotto + definite article i + piè). Mi fue is passato remoto of essere (fue) with a dative/ethical mi preceding it; here it yields the English 'it was to me' or more naturally 'it came / was beneath my feet' (i.e. the girdle came under my feet).
segments.purgatorio-4-g56: A seder is an infinitival/goal phrase ('to sit' / 'for sitting') used adverbially here; ci ponemmo is passato remoto 1st person plural of porre with the reflexive/locative ci ('we placed ourselves' = 'we sat down'). Ivi 'there' is an adverb specifying place; ambedui 'both' specifies the subject. Vòlti is a past participle (turned/facing) used predicatively with ponemmo; a levante means 'toward the east' (levante = the rising / east); ond’ (onde) = 'from which/whence' and eravam saliti (eravam = imperfect of essere + past participle saliti) conveys that 'we had climbed/come up' from that direction.
segments.purgatorio-4-g57: Che introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which' or 'for which'. Suole is present tense of suolare 'to be accustomed' (it is customary/it is wont). A riguardar is the infinitive 'to look at' (riguardar) introduced by a; giovare is an infinitive meaning 'to be of use/benefit'. Altrui 'to others' completes the idea: 'which is usually of help when one looks (i.e. looking around helps others)'. Grammatically, suole + a + infinitive expresses customary action.
segments.purgatorio-4-g58: Li occhi ('the eyes') is the direct object placed before the verb for emphasis. Prima is an adverb 'first'; drizzai is passato remoto 1st person of drizzare ('I directed/turned up'). Ai bassi liti is a prepositional phrase (a + i → ai) meaning 'to the low shores', with bassi modifying liti (plural).
segments.purgatorio-4-g59: Poscia is an adverb 'afterwards/then'. Li is the direct object pronoun 'them' (referring back to the eyes) placed before the verb. Alzai is passato remoto 1st person of alzare ('I raised'); al sole is the goal 'to the sun'.
segments.purgatorio-4-g60: E ammirava uses the imperfect ammirare ('I/he was marveling / noticed'), indicating a continuing mental reaction in the past. Che introduces the subordinate clause 'that'. Da sinistra 'from the left' is a locative phrase; n’ is the clitic form of ne ('of it' / 'by it') referring back to the light or cause, placed before the verb. Eravam feriti is essere in the imperfect (eravam) + past participle feriti ('we were struck/wounded'), giving a passive/result sense: 'we were struck from the left.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g61: Ben is an adverb 'well/clearly'. S’avvide is the passato remoto 3rd person of 'si avvedere' (s'avvide = 'he perceived/realized'), formed with the reflexive particle s’. Il poeta is the subject 'the poet'. Ch’ introduces the content clause 'that'; ïo is the archaic spelling of io 'I'. Stava is the imperfect of stare ('I was / I stood') used with an adjectival/complement expression: stupido tutto 'completely astonished' (stupido used adjectivally); al carro de la luce is a prepositional phrase 'at the chariot of the light' (de = of). Word order places the reporting verb and subject before the clause describing the poet's perception.
segments.purgatorio-4-g62: Ove is a relative/adverb meaning 'where'. Tra noi e Aquilone is a locative phrase 'between us and Aquilone' (Aquilone = the north wind). Intrava is the imperfect of intrare/entrare ('it was entering' / 'it entered continuously/was coming in'), with the subject omitted but understood from the previous noun (e.g. the chariot or the light). The imperfect intrava conveys ongoing past movement into the space between us and the north wind.
segments.purgatorio-4-g37: "Ed elli a me:" is an introductory tag for direct speech. "Ed" is a poetic variant of "e" (and); "elli" is an archaic third-person pronoun meaning 'he' (the verb 'said' is omitted but implied); the sequence "a me" means 'to me' and places Dante as the addressee.
segments.purgatorio-4-g38: "Nessun tuo passo caggia;" literally 'May no step of yours fall.' "Nessun" (no/none) modifies "passo" (here 'step'); "tuo" is the possessive 'your.' "Caggia" is an optative/jussive form (present subjunctive) of a verb meaning 'to fall' and is used here to issue a prohibition/wish: 'let none ... fall' (i.e. do not let any of your steps go astray).
segments.purgatorio-4-g39: "pur su al monte dietro a me acquista," is an instruction to move upward following the speaker. "pur" (still/indeed/just) adds insistence; "su al monte" = 'up to the mountain' ("su" = up, "al" = a + il = 'to the'); "dietro a me" = 'behind me' (follow me); "acquista" here is an imperative (2nd person singular of "acquistare" used in the sense 'advance/gain ground' or 'draw yourself up'), telling the addressee to proceed up the slope behind Virgil.
segments.purgatorio-4-g40: "fin che n’appaia alcuna scorta saggia" means 'until some prudent escort appears.' "Fin che" governs a subordinate clause and here takes a present subjunctive "appaia" (expressing anticipated appearance). "n’" is the clitic "ne" (unstressed), here functioning adverbially/pronominally in a way that links the appearance to the parties involved (not literally glossed by the supplied senses); "alcuna scorta saggia" = 'some wise/prudent escort.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g41: "Lo sommo er’ alto che vincea la vista," describes the top's height. "Lo sommo" = 'the summit/top'; "er’" is an archaic contraction of "era" (was, past); "alto" = high; "che vincea la vista" uses the imperfect "vincea" (from 'vincere' in the sense 'overcome/surpass') to mean 'so high that it overcame/was beyond the power of sight.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g42: "e la costa superba più assai / che da mezzo quadrante a centro lista." connects a comparison. "e la costa" = 'and the slope'; "superba" is an adjectival quality ('proud' or 'lofty'); "più assai" = 'much more'; "che" introduces the comparative 'than'; the phrase "da mezzo quadrante a centro lista" indicates a spatial extent ('from the mid-quadrant to the center's strip/edge'), so the whole says the slope extended much farther than that measure.
segments.purgatorio-4-g43: "Io era lasso," is a simple past-state clause: "Io" 'I'; "era" (imperfect of essere) 'was'; "lasso" 'weary/exhausted' — Dante stating his physical state before speaking.
segments.purgatorio-4-g44: "quando cominciai:" links the temporal clause. "quando" = 'when'; "cominciai" is passato remoto first-person singular of "cominciare" ('I began'), introducing the words he then spoke.
segments.purgatorio-4-g45: "«O dolce padre,"" is the opening of Dante's direct address. "O" is the exclamatory 'O/Oh'; "dolce" 'dear/sweet'; "padre" 'father' (address to Virgil).
segments.purgatorio-4-g46: "volgiti," is the imperative (2nd person singular) formed from "volgere" with the enclitic pronoun "ti" attached: 'turn yourself' / 'turn around.' The pronominal form directs the action to the addressee.
segments.purgatorio-4-g47: "e rimira com’ io rimango sol," contains another command and a descriptive clause. "e" = and; "rimira" is an imperative (2nd sing.) meaning 'look at' / 'contemplate'; "com’" is a contraction of "come" = 'how'; "io rimango" present-tense 'I remain/stay'; "sol" = 'alone.' Together: 'and look how I remain alone.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g48: "se non restai" is a short conditional clause. "se" = 'if'; "non" = 'not'; "restai" is passato remoto (I remained/was left). In context it expresses a condition ('if I did not remain/if I have not been left'), a somewhat elliptical way of pleading that he has been left alone.
segments.purgatorio-4-g49: "«Figliuol mio»," is an affectionate address. "Figliuol" (variant of "figliol/figlio") = 'son/child'; "mio" = 'my.' This is a vocative phrase addressing Dante.
segments.purgatorio-4-g50: "disse," is the simple past verb 'said' (passato remoto, 3rd sing. of "dire"). It closes the speech tag that introduces the direct quotation.
segments.purgatorio-4-g51: "«infin quivi ti tira»," is the quoted imperative. "infin" (short for "infin"/"fino") = 'as far as'; "quivi" = 'here/there' (archaic adverb); "ti" is the clitic 'yourself'; "tira" is an imperative (2nd sing.) of "tirare" meaning here 'draw yourself up' or 'pull yourself' — together: 'bring yourself up as far as here.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g52: "additandomi un balzo poco in sùe che da quel lato il poggio tutto gira." begins with a participial phrase. "additandomi" is a gerund/participle meaning 'pointing out to me' (the dative clitic "-mi" attached); "un balzo poco in su(e)" = 'a little bound/upward jump' used to indicate direction; "che" introduces a clause of explanation ('for'/'because'/'so that'); "da quel lato il poggio tutto gira" = 'from that side the hill turns completely' (gira = turns/goes round). Note: some spellings are archaic ("sùe").
segments.purgatorio-4-g104: This segment is only a punctuation mark (a period) terminating the previous line; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-4-g105: "Al" is the contraction of a + il, here meaning 'at the'. "suon di lei" literally 'sound of her' = 'her sound' or 'her voice'. "ciascun di noi" is a distributive expression 'each of us' (ciascun = each one, followed by di noi). "si torse" is reflexive: si + torse (passato remoto, 3rd singular) of torcere/torcersi, used because ciascun is treated as a singular 'each' — it means 'turned himself' / 'turned (around)'.
segments.purgatorio-4-g106: "e" = and, linking the next action. "vedemmo" is passato remoto, 1st plural of vedere, 'we saw'. "a mancina" is an adverbial phrase meaning 'to/on the left' (mancina = left side). "un gran" is the apocopic form of 'un grande' (a great/large). "petrone" is a large stone or boulder.
segments.purgatorio-4-g107: "del qual" = di + il + quale, the relative 'of which'. "né io né ei" is the negative correlative 'neither I nor he' (ei is an archaic form for 'he'). "prima" = 'before'. "s’accorse" = si + accorse (passato remoto, 3rd singular of accorgersi) meaning 'became aware / noticed' — reflexive si is contracted to s'. The verb is singular because each individual failed to notice it (literary usage).
segments.purgatorio-4-g108: "Là" is an adverb meaning 'there'. "ci" here is a reflexive/locative pronoun ('ourselves' / marking movement to that place). "traemmo" is passato remoto, 1st plural of trarre (with reflexive sense trarsi) meaning 'we drew ourselves' / 'we moved (ourselves)' — i.e. 'we went over there'. The combination indicates motion of the narrators toward the place just mentioned.
segments.purgatorio-4-g109: "e ivi eran persone": 'e' = and; 'ivi' = there; 'eran' = imperfect of essere, 'there were'; 'persone' = people. The relative clause 'che si stavano a l'ombra dietro al sasso' describes them: 'che' = who/that; 'si stavano' is reflexive + imperfect of stare meaning 'were standing' or 'were sitting/remaining'; 'a l'ombra' = 'in the shade'; 'dietro al sasso' = 'behind the rock'. The simile begins with 'come': 'come l'uom per negghienza a star si pone' — 'as a man, through negligence, places himself to stay' — note the present tense pone expresses a general or habitual action.
segments.purgatorio-4-g110: "E" = and; "un di lor" = 'one of them' (un = one, di lor = of them; lor is an archaic form of loro). This introduces a single person among the group.
segments.purgatorio-4-g111: "che" = who/that introducing a relative clause; "mi" = to me (indirect object for sembiare); "sembiava" = imperfect 3rd singular of sembiare, 'seemed'; "lasso" = adjective 'weary'. The construction 'mi sembrava lasso' = 'he seemed weary to me'.
segments.purgatorio-4-g112: "sedeva" = imperfect 3rd singular of sedere, 'he was sitting' or 'he sat'; "e" = and; "abbracciava" = imperfect of abbracciare, 'he embraced' or 'he had his arms around'; "le ginocchia" = 'the knees' (definite article used; refers to his own knees). The two imperfect verbs describe simultaneous, ongoing posture.
segments.purgatorio-4-g113: "tenendo" is a gerund of tenere, 'holding'; "'l viso" = il viso, 'the face' (apostrophe marks the elision of i); "giù" = down; "tra esse" = 'among/between them' (esse refers back to the feminine plural 'ginocchia'); "basso" = low. The whole participial phrase modifies the man: 'holding his face down low between them.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g130: This segment contains only a single punctuation mark (a period) in the source; there is no lexical content to analyze or translate.
segments.purgatorio-4-g131: Li is the archaic masculine plural definite article (modern Italian i/gli) introducing the compound subject. 'Li atti suoi pigri e le corte parole' is a coordinate plural subject ('his sluggish gestures and his short words'). 'Mosser' is a literary/past (passato remoto) third-person plural form meaning 'they moved' (poetic variant of mossero), so the verb agrees with the plural subject. 'Le labbra mie' is the direct object with a possessive after the noun (typical older word order), and 'un poco a riso' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'a little into a smile' (a riso = into a smile), expressing the result of the movement.
segments.purgatorio-4-g132: 'Poi' is an adverb meaning 'then' or 'afterward.' 'Cominciai' is the first-person singular passato remoto (I began), which here introduces the following direct speech; the colon that follows signals that Dante himself starts speaking.
segments.purgatorio-4-g133: A simple vocative: the proper name 'Belacqua' addresses the person directly. In context this is a direct call to that character rather than a descriptive noun.
segments.purgatorio-4-g134: 'A me' is a topical indirect-object phrase for emphasis ('to me' / 'as for me'). 'Non dole' uses 'dole' (from dolere) in the third-person singular present sense 'it pains' or 'it grieves,' so 'a me non dole di te' literally means 'it does not grieve me about you.' 'Omai' (= by now/anymore) marks that this lack of pain is a current, settled state.
segments.purgatorio-4-g135: 'Ma' is the coordinating conjunction 'but.' 'Dimmi' is the second-person singular imperative with clitic object 'mi' (tell me); it addresses Belacqua directly and requests information that follows.
segments.purgatorio-4-g136: 'Perché' introduces a direct interrogative ('why'). 'Assiso' is a past participle used adjectivally meaning 'seated' or 'sitting'; it precedes 'quiritto,' an archaic/poetic word here meaning 'upright' or 'straight' (not the literal 'Quirite' sense). 'Se’' is the contracted second-person singular present of 'essere' (modern sei) meaning 'you are.' Together: 'why are you sitting upright?'
segments.purgatorio-4-g137: 'Attendi' is the second-person singular present indicative of 'attendere' ('do you wait/expect'). The subject pronoun 'tu' is explicit for emphasis (you). 'Iscorta' is a noun meaning 'escort' or 'retinue' (here 'an escort' that one might be awaiting). The order verb–subject–object is used for rhetorical emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-4-g138: 'O pur' is a conjunctive phrase meaning roughly 'or else' / 'or perhaps.' 'Lo modo usato' = 'the way/manner used' (i.e., your accustomed manner or the way you are used to behaving). 'T’ ha’ ripriso' uses the auxiliary 'ha' + past participle 'ripriso' (perfect tense) with the clitic 't’' (to you): 'has reproved you' or 'has made you ashamed/rebuked you.' The whole alternative question asks whether he is waiting for an escort or whether his habitual manner has somehow rebuked or checked him.
segments.purgatorio-4-g139: This segment is only a punctuation mark (a period) in the source and contains no lexical material to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-4-g1: 'Quando' introduces a temporal clause ('when'); 'per dilettanze' = 'through/by delights' (dilettanze plural); 'o ver' is an older/poetic short form of 'o vero' meaning 'or rather/indeed'; 'per doglie' = 'through pains/griefs'. Word order is a series of adverbial phrases setting the circumstances.
segments.purgatorio-4-g2: 'che' introduces a subordinate clause ('that'); 'alcuna' = 'any/some' (indefinite); 'virtù' here means a faculty or moral power ('virtue'); 'nostra' qualifies it as 'our'; 'comprenda' is 3rd person singular present subjunctive of 'comprendere' used in a relative clause meaning 'takes hold of/includes/grasps'.
segments.purgatorio-4-g3: 'L’anima' is the subject ('the soul'); 'bene' is an adverb 'rightly/well'; 'ad essa' = 'to it' (the feminine pronoun 'essa' referring back to the virtue); 'si raccoglie' is the reflexive present indicative of 'raccogliersi' meaning 'gathers itself' or 'turns itself inward' toward that virtue. The reflexive verb shows the soul's inward movement.
segments.purgatorio-4-g4: 'Par' = 'it seems' (impersonal present of 'parere'); 'ch’' (= che) introduces a subordinate clause governed by 'par'; 'a nulla potenza' = 'to/for no power' (nulla = no/none); 'più intenda' uses 'intenda' (subjunctive because of 'par che') from 'intendere' meaning here 'attend to / give attention to'. Together: 'it seems that it attends to no other power.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g5: 'E questo è contra quello error' = 'and this is contrary to that error'; 'che crede' = 'which believes/thinks'; the second clause 'ch’un’anima sovr’ altra in noi s’accenda' is reported content: 'that one soul over another in us kindles' — 's’accenda' is subjunctive (after 'crede che') and uses 'accendere' figuratively 'to kindle/arouse'. 'sovr'' = 'sopra' (over/on/above).
segments.purgatorio-4-g6: 'E' = 'and'; 'però' = 'therefore' or 'so' — a connective adverb introducing the conclusion from the previous statement. Short, linking phrase.
segments.purgatorio-4-g7: 'quando' introduces a temporal clause ('when'); 's’ode' = 'si ode' impersonal 'one hears' (or 'is heard'); 'cosa' = 'something'; 'o vede' = 'or sees'; the relative clause 'che tegna forte a sé l’anima volta' = 'that holds strongly to itself the soul turned (toward it)' — 'tegna' (from 'tegnere/tenere') = 'holds/keeps', 'forte' = 'strongly', 'a sé' = 'to itself', 'l’anima volta' = 'the soul turned/turned toward it' (volta = 'turned/turning').
segments.purgatorio-4-g8: 'Vassene' is an older/pronominal form meaning 'se ne va / goes away' — here 'vassene 'l tempo' = 'time passes away'; 'e l’uom non se n’avvede' = 'and the man does not perceive it' ('se n'avvede' = 'si ne avvede', 'notice / become aware of it'). This clause states the practical result: while the soul is held, time slips by unnoticed.
segments.purgatorio-4-g9: 'Ch’ altra potenza è quella che l’ascolta' = 'For another power is that which hears it.' 'Ch’ ' = 'che' introducing an explanatory clause; 'altra potenza' = 'another power/faculty'; 'quella' = 'that one'; 'che l’ascolta' = 'which listens to/hears it' (l' refers to whatever holds the soul).
segments.purgatorio-4-g10: 'E altra è quella c’ha l’anima intera' = 'And another is that which has the whole soul.' 'c’ ha' is contracted 'che ha' (which has/possesses); 'l’anima intera' = 'the entire soul' — Dante distinguishes the faculty that listens from the faculty that possesses the whole soul.
segments.purgatorio-4-g11: 'Questa è quasi legata e quella è sciolta' = 'This one is almost bound and that one is loose.' 'questa/quella' refer back to the two powers just described; 'legata' = 'bound/tied' (past participle used predicatively), 'sciolta' = 'loosened/released'.
segments.purgatorio-4-g12: 'Di ciò' = 'of this/about this'; 'ebb’ io' is an archaic contracted past ('ebbi io' / 'I had') — 'ebb'' here marks past possession/experience; 'esperïenza vera' = 'true/real experience'. The order is emphatic: object + auxiliary + subject + noun.
segments.purgatorio-4-g13: 'Udendo' and 'ammirando' are gerunds ('hearing' and 'marveling'), describing circumstances under which the speaker had that experience; 'quello spirto' = 'that spirit' (the spirit they were listening to). The two gerunds frame the verb in the previous segment.
segments.purgatorio-4-g14: 'Ché' = 'for/because' (introducing an explanatory remark); 'ben' is an intensifier 'indeed/actually'; 'cinquanta gradi' = 'fifty degrees'; 'salito era' = 'had risen' (past perfect: past participle 'salito' with auxiliary 'era'). 'Lo sole' = 'the sun.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g15: 'E io non m’era accorto' = 'and I had not noticed / I was not aware' — 'm’era accorto' is an analytic past form ('mi era accorto') meaning 'I had perceived/been aware.' The negative 'non' negates the state of awareness.
segments.purgatorio-4-g16: 'Quando venimmo' = 'when we came/reached'; 'ove' = 'where/at which place'; 'quell’ anime ad una' = 'those souls at one/at the same time' (ad una = all together/at once); 'gridaro a noi' = past 'they cried out to us.' This places the moment when the souls addressed them.
segments.purgatorio-4-g17: Direct speech: 'Qui' = 'here'; 'è' = 'is'; 'vostro' = 'your'; 'dimando' is an archaic form (related to 'domanda' = 'request/question' or 'demand'); the phrase addresses the pilgrims with 'Here is your request'.
segments.purgatorio-4-g18: This long descriptive phrase names a place/feature: 'Maggiore aperta' = 'the larger opening' (Maggiore as substantive + aperta 'open'); 'molte volte' = 'many times'; 'impruna' is a place-name; 'con una forcatella di sue spine' = 'with a small fork (forcatella, diminutive) of its thorns' (forcatella can be literal small fork or a small forked path); 'l’uom de la villa' = 'the man of the village' (the villager); 'quando l’uva imbruna' = 'when the grape darkens/turns ripe' (imbruna = becomes dark). The phrase is tenor-heavy: modifiers precede the human subject ('l’uom').
segments.purgatorio-4-g19: 'Che non era la calla' = 'which was not the path/track' ('calla' = narrow way/path); 'onde salìne lo duca mio' = 'from which my guide climbed/ascended' — 'salìne' is an archaic past form meaning 'rose/climbed from it' and 'lo duca mio' = 'my guide' (Virgil). The clause excludes that route as the one they used.
segments.purgatorio-4-g20: 'E io appresso' = 'and I after / and I close behind' — 'appresso' is an adverb meaning 'after, near, behind', indicating Dante followed Virgil closely.
segments.purgatorio-4-g21: 'Soli' = 'alone' (plural to agree with 'lo duca mio e io' understood in the context): terse emphatic note that they were alone when contrasted with the larger company.
segments.purgatorio-4-g22: 'Come da noi la schiera si partìne' = 'as the troop/host departed from us.' 'Come da noi' = 'as/from us'; 'la schiera' = 'the array/company'; 'si partìne' is an archaic past form meaning 'left'/'departed' (reflexive particle preserved). The clause explains that they were left alone as the group went on.
segments.purgatorio-4-g89: Ed is a simple coordinating conjunction 'and' used to link speech to what follows. 'elli' is an archaic/dialectal third-person subject pronoun (functioning here as 'he'); 'a me' is the prepositional phrase 'to me' marking the indirect object (a + accusative pronoun).
segments.purgatorio-4-g90: A straightforward predication: 'Questa montagna' is the subject noun phrase ('this mountain'), 'è' is the present indicative copula 'is', and 'tale' is the predicate adjective meaning 'such/of such a sort'.
segments.purgatorio-4-g91: The conjunction 'che' introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause. 'al cominciar' is a nominalized infinitive with the contracted article (a + il = al) meaning 'at the beginning'; 'di sotto' is a locative complement 'from/below'; the clause closes with 'è grave' where 'grave' is a predicate adjective meaning 'burdensome/difficult'.
segments.purgatorio-4-g92: This is the first half of a comparative correlative: 'e quant’... più ... va sù' = 'and the more ... goes up'. 'quant’' (short for quanto) combines with 'più' to form 'the more...', with 'va sù' ('goes up') as the verb phrase. The subject 'om' (man/one) is implied in the correlative sense.
segments.purgatorio-4-g93: 'e' continues the sentence ('and'); 'men' is an archaic comparative 'less' (modern meno); 'fa male' is a fixed idiom 'does harm/causes pain' — together: 'and it causes less harm' = 'and it hurts less.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g94: Però is a discourse connective meaning 'therefore' (or 'however' depending on context); here it introduces the consequence to follow.
segments.purgatorio-4-g95: 'quand’' (quando) begins a temporal clause 'when'; 'ella' (here referring to the mountain) is the subject 'it/that thing'; 'ti' is the indirect object 'to you'; 'parrà' is future indicative of parere ('will seem'); 'soave' is the predicative adjective 'pleasant/gentle'; 'tanto' intensifies ('so much/so').
segments.purgatorio-4-g96: 'che' introduces the result/comparison clause: 'sù andar ti fia leggero' is built with a nominalized infinitive 'sù andar' ('going up') as the logical subject, 'ti' marking to whom it will be light, 'fia' an older/fixed form meaning 'will be', and 'leggero' the predicate adjective 'light/easy'. The comparative 'com’ a seconda giù andar per nave' follows: 'com’' = 'as/like', 'a seconda' is a nautical idiom meaning 'with a following/favorable wind', and 'giù andar per nave' = 'going down by ship' (per = 'by').
segments.purgatorio-4-g97: 'allor' = 'then'; 'sarai' is future 2nd person singular of essere ('you will be'); 'al fin d’esto sentiero' is a locative phrase 'at the end of this path' (al = a + il; fin = end/as far as; d’ = di 'of'; 'esto' = 'this').
segments.purgatorio-4-g98: 'quivi' is locative 'there'; 'di' introduces the infinitive ('di riposar' = 'for resting' / 'to rest'); 'l’affanno' is the direct object meaning 'the toil/affliction/fatigue'; 'aspetta' is present indicative 3rd singular 'awaits' (so: 'there awaits rest for the toil' / 'there expect to rest from your strain').
segments.purgatorio-4-g99: 'Più non rispondo' is an absolute negative statement meaning 'I no longer answer' or 'I answer no more'; 'più' here has the value 'any longer' and precedes the negation 'non', with 'rispondo' 1st-person present 'I answer'.
segments.purgatorio-4-g100: Simple coordination: 'e questo so' = 'and this I know'; 'so' is 1st-person singular of sapere ('I know'); 'per vero' is an idiomatic intensifier 'for true' = 'for certain/indeed'.
segments.purgatorio-4-g101: E com' elli ebbe sua parola detta is a temporal clause: 'E com (come) elli ebbe ... detta' = 'And when he had said his word.' 'Com'' is an elided form of 'come' (here meaning 'when/as'); 'elli' is an archaic subject pronoun (he) used here; 'ebbe' is the passato remoto of avere and with the past participle 'detta' forms a perfect sense 'had said'. Word order places the participial phrase after the auxiliary for a succinct poetic clause.
segments.purgatorio-4-g102: Una voce di presso son�f2 is a simple main clause: 'a voice sounded nearby.' 'di presso' is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'nearby' or 'close by'; 'son�f2' is the passato remoto (3rd sing.) of sonare meaning 'sounded'. The noun phrase 'una voce' is the grammatical subject.
segments.purgatorio-4-g103: The quoted exclamation Forse che di sedere in pria avrai distretta! is a rhetorical prediction. 'Forse che' introduces a conjecture ('perhaps that...'); 'di sedere in pria' is an infinitive phrase introduced by 'di' (here functioning like 'from' or marking the action 'to sit / sitting first/front'); 'avrai' is the future indicative of avere ('you will have') and with the past participle 'distretta' expresses a resultant/state sense (older usage where avere + participle conveys 'will be/shall find yourself constrained'). So the whole means roughly 'perhaps you will be constrained/prevented from sitting first.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g151: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking this clause to what precedes. Già is an adverb meaning 'already' placed before the subject for emphasis. Il poeta is the subject; innanzi + mi (innanzi mi) means 'before me' / 'in front of me' with innanzi functioning adverbially and mi the clitic object referring to the speaker. Saliva is the imperfect indicative of salire ('was ascending' / 'went up'), used here narratively to indicate repeated or continuous past action.
segments.purgatorio-4-g152: e is the coordinating 'and' continuing the narrative; dicea is the imperfect of dire ('he was saying' / 'he said repeatedly'), used to introduce the following direct speech — a typical narrative device in Dante.
segments.purgatorio-4-g153: Vienne is an archaic/poetic form of the imperative or hortative meaning 'come (along)' (cf. modern vieni); it is used to urge the addressee to move. Omai is an adverb meaning 'now' / 'by now', reinforcing immediacy.
segments.purgatorio-4-g154: Vedi is the imperative 'see' or 'notice' (2nd singular), introducing what to observe. Ch' is the clitic form of che = 'that'. È + tocco functions as a perfect/passive sense 'has been touched' or 'has reached' (è = present of essere used with the participial form tocco here in older/poetic usage). Meridïan dal sole = 'the meridian of the sun' (i.e. midday). E introduces the second coordinated clause: a la riva is a locative phrase 'at the shore'; cuopre is present indicative 3rd singular 'covers' and the subject la notte ('the night') follows the verb because of poetic inversion; già 'already' modifies cuopre; col = con + il 'with the'; piè 'foot' is figurative; Morrocco in apposition names or locates that foot (a poetic personification indicating that night has already reached as far as Morocco).
segments.purgatorio-4-g63: Ond’ is a contracted form of onde meaning 'whereupon' or 'thereupon'; elli here is the archaic subject pronoun 'he' (Virgil/the speaker), and a me marks the indirect object 'to me' — the whole is the spoken formula 'Thereupon he to me:' introducing reported speech.
segments.purgatorio-4-g64: This is the protasis of a conditional. Se introduces an if-clause; fossero is the imperfect subjunctive of essere, used for a hypothetical/contrary-to-fact situation ('if they were'); in compagnia di quello specchio is a prepositional phrase modifying the subject, and the relative clause che sù e giù del suo lume conduce describes that mirror ('which conducts its light up and down').
segments.purgatorio-4-g65: Vedresti is the present conditional second-person singular ('you would see'), the result clause dependent on the previous if. The construction is verb of perception + infinitive (vedresti ... rotare): 'you would see ... turn.' Il Zodïaco rubecchio places the descriptive adjective after the noun ('the somewhat reddish Zodiac'), ancora 'still', and a l’Orse più stretto rotare expresses direction/degree ('turn more tightly about the Bears').
segments.purgatorio-4-g66: This is the exception clause introduced by se non (i.e. 'unless'). Uscisse is the imperfect subjunctive of uscire used in this subordinate clause ('should go out' / 'went out'); fuor del cammin vecchio is a prepositional phrase 'outside the old path/course.' The whole means 'unless it left the ancient course.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g67: Come ciò sia is a fixed phrase meaning roughly 'however that may be' or 'how this is' introducing an explanation; sia is present subjunctive of essere used to mark possibility or concession.
segments.purgatorio-4-g68: Se begins a conditional ('if'); vuoi (from volere) is second-person singular present 'you wish/ want'; poter pensare is the infinitive chain 'to be able to think/imagine.' The sense is 'if you wish to be able to imagine it.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g69: Dentro raccolto is an adverbial phrase meaning 'with (your) thoughts gathered within' — dentro ('within') plus raccolto (past participle/adjective 'collected, composed'), instructing mental concentration before the act of imagining.
segments.purgatorio-4-g70: Imagina is an imperative (or hortative) 'imagine' directing the hearer to form a mental picture; Sïòn is the object. Con questo monte in su la terra stare is an unusual word order: stare ('to be situated') takes the locative phrase 'on the earth' (in su la terra) with con questo monte ('together with this mountain'). Sì introduces the result clause that follows ('so that').
segments.purgatorio-4-g71: Ch’ (che) introduces the result or explanatory clause: amendue (archaic 'ambedue') 'both'; hanno (present indicative) 'have'; un solo orizzòn 'a single horizon'; e diversi emisperi 'and different hemispheres.' Word order is normal but contains archaic forms (amendue).
segments.purgatorio-4-g72: Onde ('whence'/'therefore') links consequence: la strada 'the road' is the subject of the following relative clause. Mal non seppe uses seppe (passato remoto of sapere) with negation to mean 'did not manage' or 'could hardly'; carreggiar Fetòn ('to carry/bring Phaethon') — here a mythological allusion to Phaethon. The whole says roughly 'whence (i.e. therefore) the road which Phaethon could ill manage.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g73: Vedrai is future second-person singular 'you will see'; come introduces the manner; a costui 'for this one' (to this person/thing). Convien is the impersonal/present form meaning 'it is necessary' and requires a subjunctive: che vada ('that he/it go') — vada is present subjunctive of andare after convien. Da l’un indicates 'from the one [side]'.
segments.purgatorio-4-g74: Quando is temporal/subordinate 'when'; the short clause a colui da l’altro fianco specifies the counterpart movement: 'when (he) (goes) to that one from the other flank.' The verb is understood from the previous clause (ellipsis).
segments.purgatorio-4-g75: This final conditional clause begins with se ('if') and issues the condition for understanding: lo 'it' (direct object) è 'ntelletto tuo 'your intellect/understanding' — ben chiaro 'well and clearly' — bada is the present imperative 'pay heed' or 'take care.' So: 'if your intellect pays it careful clear attention.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g76: Certo here is an interjection 'certainly' or 'surely' introducing the speaker's assent.
segments.purgatorio-4-g77: Maestro mio is a vocative phrase 'my master' (direct address).
segments.purgatorio-4-g78: Diss' io is the narrator's formula 'I said.' Diss' is an archaic contracted past form (dissi/dicei); io is the subject pronoun 'I.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g79: This clause expresses a negated temporal statement: unquanco (archaic adverb) 'ever/at any time'; non vid' io (vid' = vidi/diedit) 'I did not see' or 'never saw'; chiaro 'clearly'; sì com' io discerno 'as I discern/ perceive'; là dove mio ingegno parea manco 'there where my wit seemed lacking.' The order and archaic forms emphasize contrast between present clarity and past lack.
segments.purgatorio-4-g80: Che introduces a relative clause identifying the thing perceived: ’l mezzo cerchio del moto superno = 'the middle circle of the heavenly motion.' Word order is noun phrase with article and adjectives; superno is an adjective 'heavenly/upper.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g81: Another relative clause: che si chiama Equatore 'which is called Equator' uses the impersonal reflexive si chiama (lit. 'is called'); in alcun' arte means 'in some art/discipline' (i.e. in certain branches of learning or technical usage).
segments.purgatorio-4-g82: E che sempre riman tra ’l sole e ’l verno: 'and which always remains between the Sun and the Winter.' Riman is present indicative 'remains'; tra ... e ... marks the interval/position. Verbo 'verno' here refers to winter/seasonal direction.
segments.purgatorio-4-g83: Per la ragion che di’ — 'for the reason that I have said.' Per la ragion 'for the reason' and che introduces 'that which'; di’ is an archaic shortened form meaning 'I have said' or 'I said' (the first-person reference to the previous explanation).
segments.purgatorio-4-g84: Quinci si parte 'from there it departs/sets off': quinci ('from here/on that side'), si parte is impersonal/passive-like 'it parts/sets off'; verso settentrïon gives the direction 'toward the north.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g85: Quanto li Ebrei vedevan lui verso la calda parte: quanto here means 'as' or 'to the extent that'; li Ebrei vedevan (imperfect 'the Hebrews used to see') lui (it/that, here the circle) verso la calda parte 'toward the warm side.' Vedevan is imperfect descriptive past.
segments.purgatorio-4-g86: Ma se a te piace = 'but if it pleases you' or 'if you like' — a polite conditional opening. Ma 'but', se 'if', a te piace 'it pleases you / you wish.'
segments.purgatorio-4-g87: Volontier saprei is a polite, conditional formula: volontier 'gladly', saprei is the first-person conditional of sapere ('I would know' / 'I would like to know'); quanto avemo ad andar 'how far we have to go' — avemo is an archaic present 'we have' (abbiamo) and ad andar is a periphrastic infinitive 'to go'.
segments.purgatorio-4-g88: Ché gives the reason 'for' or 'for the reason that'; ’l poggio sale 'the hill/slope rises' (sale present indicative); più che ... non posson is a comparative construction 'more than ... can' — salir non posson li occhi miei: 'my eyes cannot climb (that high).' Posson is present of potere (they can), and li occhi miei is the logical subject of that inability.
segments.purgatorio-4-g140: "Ed elli:" is a short reporting clause. "Ed" = and; "elli" is an archaic/poetic third-person pronoun meaning "he" used to introduce the speaker.
segments.purgatorio-4-g141: "«O frate,"" is the vocative address. "O" is the vocative/exclamatory particle ("O/Oh"); "frate" is the vocative "brother" (not necessarily a literal friar, but a familiar address).
segments.purgatorio-4-g142: This is an elliptical interrogative. "andar in sù" = "to go upward" (infinitive + adverbial phrase); "che" here is an interrogative/what-word ("what"/"what does ..."); "porta" is 3rd-person singular present of "portare" meaning "brings/ leads to." Word order places the finite verb at the end for the question.
segments.purgatorio-4-g143: "ché" = "for/because" introducing the reason. The main verb "lascerebbe" is a conditional ("would let/allow") from the archaic verb form "lascare" (modern "lasciare"); "mi" = "me" is the indirect object; "ire" is the infinitive "to go" (an older infinitive form); "a’ martìri" = "to the torments" ("a" = to, the apostrophe shows older orthography). The final relative clause "l'angel di Dio che siede in su la porta" = "the angel of God who sits upon the gate," where "che" is a relative pronoun and "siede" = "sits/is seated."
segments.purgatorio-4-g144: "Prima convien che ..." is an impersonal construction: "Prima" = first; "convien" = it is necessary/fitting; "che" introduces a subordinate clause requiring the subjunctive. "m'aggiri" is a subjunctive form ("that it turn me/that heaven turn me") and "di fuor da essa" = "outside of it." The order places the necessity word before the clause and the pronoun clitic before the verb.
segments.purgatorio-4-g145: "quanto fece in vita" modifies how much heaven must turn him: "quanto" = "as much/as far as"; "fece" is the passato remoto (he did) of "fare" meaning "it did"; "in vita" = "in (his) life". This is a comparative clause linked back to "tanto" in the previous segment.
segments.purgatorio-4-g146: "per ch'io 'ndugiai al fine i buon sospiri" gives the reason: "per" = "because/for"; "ch'io 'ndugiai" = "that I delayed" ("'ndugiai" = archaic first-person past of "indugiare", "to delay"); "al fine" = "to the end/until the end"; "i buon sospiri" = "the good sighs" (direct object).
segments.purgatorio-4-g147: "se orazïone in prima non m'aita che surga sù di cuor che in grazia viva;" reads as a conditional/explanatory clause: "se" = "if"; "orazione in prima" = "a first prayer"; "non m'aita" = "does not help me" ("aita" = present of an older verb "aita(re)"); the following "che" introduces the desired/resultant condition: "che surga sù di cuor che in grazia viva" = "that it may rise up from a heart that lives in grace." "surga" is subjunctive (purpose/result: 'so that it rise').
segments.purgatorio-4-g148: "l'altra che val," = "the other one that counts/that is of value." "L'altra" = "the other (prayer)"; "che" = relative "that"; "val" is the present of "valere" (= "is worth/avails") used here in the sense "matters/has value."
segments.purgatorio-4-g149: "che 'n ciel non è udita?" is a relative clause/question about the prayer just mentioned. "che" = "which/that" (relative); "'n" is a contraction of "in" (old orthography) meaning "in"; "ciel" = "heaven"; "non è udita" = "is not heard" ("è" + past participle "udita" = "is heard" — negative asks whether it has not been heard in heaven).
segments.purgatorio-4-g150: This segment contains only a period (full stop). It carries no lexical material and serves purely as punctuation marking the end of the sentence or a pause in the text; there are no verbs, nouns, or grammatical forms to analyze here.

sentences.purgatorio-4-s5: One goes to San Leo and descends to Noli; one mounts up to Bismantova and to the summit with its feet; but here one must fly, I mean with the nimble wings and with the feathers of great desire, following behind that guide which hope gave me and made light.
sentences.purgatorio-4-s6: We clambered within the broken rock, and on every side the brink pressed upon us, and the ground below demanded both feet and hands.
sentences.purgatorio-4-s7: After we were upon the highest rim of the high bank, onto the exposed slope, 'My master,' I said, 'which way shall we take?'
sentences.purgatorio-4-s29: “O my sweet lord,” I said, “look at that one who shows himself more negligent than if laziness were his sister.”
sentences.purgatorio-4-s30: Then he turned to us and fixed his mind (on us), moving his face up along the thigh, and said: “Now go up, for you are able!”
sentences.purgatorio-4-s32: Then I recognized who he was, and that anguish which still somewhat quickened my breath did not prevent me from going to him; and afterwards, when I had reached him, he barely raised his head, saying: "Have you well seen how the sun from the left shoulder leads the chariot?"
sentences.purgatorio-4-s13: His words so spurred me on that I strained after him, clutching close to him, until the girdle was beneath my feet.
sentences.purgatorio-4-s14: There we both sat down, turned toward the east from which we had climbed.
sentences.purgatorio-4-s15: First I turned my eyes to the low shores; then I raised them to the sun, and I marveled that we were struck from the left.
sentences.purgatorio-4-s16: The poet well perceived that I stood, entirely stupefied at the chariot of the light, where it entered between us and the north wind.
sentences.purgatorio-4-s9: And he to me: "Let none of your steps fall; rather, go up the mountain behind me, until some prudent escort appear."
sentences.purgatorio-4-s10: The summit was so high that it outstripped sight, and the proud slope far more than from the mid-quadrant to the center's strip.
sentences.purgatorio-4-s11: I was weary, when I began: "O sweet father, turn and look how I remain alone, if I did not remain."
sentences.purgatorio-4-s12: "My son," he said, "draw yourself up as far as here," pointing out to me with a little bound upward, for from that side the hill turns all the way around.
sentences.purgatorio-4-s26: At her sound each of us turned, and we saw on the left a great boulder, of which neither I nor he had noticed before.
sentences.purgatorio-4-s27: There we moved over; and there were people who stood in the shade behind the rock, as a man, through negligence, places himself to remain.
sentences.purgatorio-4-s28: And one of them, who seemed to me weary, sat and clasped his knees, holding his face down low between them.
sentences.purgatorio-4-s34: His sluggish gestures and short words moved my lips into a slight smile; then I began: "Belacqua, I no longer grieve for you; but tell me: why are you sitting upright?"
sentences.purgatorio-4-s35: Are you waiting for an escort, or has your accustomed manner reproved you?
sentences.purgatorio-4-s1: When, through delights or rather through pains, any virtue of ours takes hold, the soul rightly withdraws itself to that (virtue), and it seems that it pays no attention to any other power; and this is contrary to that error which believes that one soul kindles itself upon another in us.
sentences.purgatorio-4-s2: And therefore, when one hears or sees something that strongly holds the soul turned to itself, time slips away and the man does not notice it; for another power is that which listens to it, and another is that which has the whole soul: this one is almost bound and that one is loosened.
sentences.purgatorio-4-s3: Of this I had true experience, hearing that spirit and marveling; for the sun had indeed risen fifty degrees, and I had not noticed it, when we came to where those souls all together cried out to us: "Here is your request."
sentences.purgatorio-4-s4: The larger opening at Impruna, many times traversed by the villager with a small fork (of its thorns) when the grape darkens, which was not the path by which my guide and I had climbed, I following close behind, alone, as the company parted from us.
sentences.purgatorio-4-s21: And he said to me: "This mountain is such that it is always hard at the beginning from below; and the more one goes up, the less it hurts."
sentences.purgatorio-4-s22: Therefore, when it shall seem so pleasant to you that going up will be light, like going down by ship with a following wind, then you will be at the end of this path; there expect relief from your toil.
sentences.purgatorio-4-s23: I answer no more, and this I know for certain."
sentences.purgatorio-4-s24: And when he had said his word, a voice sounded nearby: "Perhaps you will be prevented from sitting first!"
sentences.purgatorio-4-s41: And already the poet was ascending before me, and he said: "Come now; see that the sun has touched the meridian, and at the shore Night already covers Morocco with her foot."
sentences.purgatorio-4-s17: Then he said to me: “If Castor and Pollux were in the company of that mirror which carries its light up and down, you would see the ruddy Zodiac still turning ever more closely about the Bears, unless it went outside its old course.”
sentences.purgatorio-4-s18: How that may be — if you wish to be able to think it, with your mind gathered within — imagine Zion and this mountain standing on the earth so that both have one single horizon but different hemispheres; hence the road that Phaethon could scarcely travel, you will see how it is necessary for this one to go from the one side when the other goes from the opposite flank, if your intellect pays it careful, clear attention.”
sentences.purgatorio-4-s19: “Certainly, my master,” I said, “never have I seen as clearly — where my intellect seemed wanting — that middle circle of the supernal motion, which some call the Equator, and which always lies between the Sun and the Winter; for the reason I have given, from there it departs toward the north, as the Hebrews saw it toward the warm side.”
sentences.purgatorio-4-s20: “But if it pleases you, I would gladly know how far we must go; for the slope rises higher than my eyes can climb.”
sentences.purgatorio-4-s37: And he said: "O brother, what does going up there lead to?"
sentences.purgatorio-4-s38: For the angel of God who sits upon the gate would not allow me to go to the torments.
sentences.purgatorio-4-s39: First it is necessary that Heaven turn me so far outside it as it did in life, because I delayed the good sighs until the end — if the first prayer does not help me, so that it may rise up from a heart living in grace; the other one that counts, which has not been heard in Heaven?
